title: Secrets Don’t Belong in Config
excerpt: An audit of 445 NixOS modules shows the cost of making configuration and secrets share one interface.
content_html:
Jul 20, 2026

Applications should not require passwords, API keys, or tokens in their configuration files.

Configuration describes behavior. It belongs in git, code review, bug reports, and developer machines.

A secret grants authority. It needs restricted access and independent rotation.

Putting both in one file couples different lifecycles and audiences. If rotating a password requires regenerating application configuration, the interface has coupled them too tightly.

NixOS contains 110 workarounds for this

Section titled “NixOS contains 110 workarounds for this”

We audited all 445 NixOS modules that handle a real secret in nixpkgs at commit 141f212, classifying each by where its secret value ends up.

Where the secret value ends upModulesShare
Merged into a config file at runtime11025%
Inlined into a config in /nix/store429%
Delivered as an environment variable16136%
Left in a dedicated file opened by the app5813%
Loaded through systemd credentials5312%
Passed as a command-line argument194%
Classification uncertain2—

The interesting number is 110. A quarter of the modules retrieve a secret safely, then copy it into configuration because that is the only interface the application accepts.

These modules use envsubst, replace-secret, jq, yq, sed, or custom code to assemble a restricted file at startup. The result can be secure, but every module now owns application-specific, security-sensitive glue just to combine two inputs that should have remained separate.

This is not unique to NixOS. The same workaround appears as an entrypoint script, Helm template, init container, or CI interpolation step on other platforms.

As a side note, 42 modules can inline secrets into the world-readable /nix/store. That direct security problem is tracked in nixpkgs issue #24288. The 110 runtime mergers make the broader point: even when deployment authors avoid the leak, the missing separation still creates work.

Give secrets their own interface

Section titled “Give secrets their own interface”

Applications should accept secret values through a dedicated runtime channel, such as:

  • a password_file or token_file setting;
  • a systemd credential;
  • a narrowly scoped environment variable;
  • or an external secret provider.

These mechanisms are not equally safe: environment variables can be inherited, arguments can appear in process listings, and files still need correct permissions. What separation does guarantee is that the deployer no longer has to manufacture a second, secret-bearing version of the configuration.

The principle is simple; implementing it across environments is not. Local development might use a system keyring, CI environment variables, and production 1Password or Vault. Without a shared abstraction, each environment needs its own naming, lookup, validation, and injection glue.

How I got it wrong in Cachix

Section titled “How I got it wrong in Cachix”

Cachix historically stored its auth token and per-cache signing keys in ~/.config/cachix/cachix.dhall, alongside cache names and other configuration. It was convenient, but the file had to be treated as a secret even though much of it was ordinary configuration.

A typical file mixed them directly:

The cache name is configuration; the auth token and signing key are secrets. You could not share the cache configuration without also sharing credentials.

devenv 2.2 separates the token through SecretSpec. The project declares CACHIX_AUTH_TOKEN, devenv resolves it from the configured provider, and the value is passed to Cachix without being added to devenv’s configuration.

Cachix PR #737 brings the same boundary into the client through the SecretSpec Haskell SDK. It resolves CACHIX_AUTH_TOKEN and CACHIX_SIGNING_KEY from SecretSpec and can store them in the user’s chosen provider instead of cachix.dhall. Existing environment variables and config files remain higher-priority fallbacks for compatibility. The PR is still open and is not available in a released Cachix version yet.

That is the problem SecretSpec is designed to solve: configuration declares the requirement, while each environment chooses where the value lives.

Declare once, resolve anywhere

Section titled “Declare once, resolve anywhere”

SecretSpec applies that separation by making secretspec.toml a declaration of what an application needs, without storing the values:

Providers decide where the values live. A developer can use the system keyring, CI can use environment variables, and production can use 1Password, Vault/OpenBao, or a cloud secret manager without changing the declaration.

An existing application can receive the resolved values at startup:

Applications can also resolve them directly through the SecretSpec SDKs for Rust, Python, Go, Ruby, Node.js/TypeScript, Haskell, PHP, and C#, all sharing the same resolver so behavior stays consistent across languages.

Providers own where secret values come from. SDKs give applications an idiomatic way to consume them. Configuration remains a shareable declaration of what is required.

Making the boundary practical

Section titled “Making the boundary practical”

If you maintain an application, stop adding passwords and tokens to ordinary configuration schemas. Accept a file reference, credential, environment variable, or provider instead.

For NixOS, SecretSpec issue #65 tracks how an official integration could declare and resolve secrets without per-module substitution glue.

Consistent secret handling across developer machines, CI, and production used to require infrastructure that only dedicated platform teams could build. A project of any size should be able to separate secrets from configuration without building its own secrets platform first.

These modules use envsubst, replace-secret, jq, yq, sed, or custom code to assemble a restricted file at startup. The result can be secure, but every module now owns application-specific, security-sensitive glue just to combine two inputs that should have remained separate. This is not unique to NixOS. The same workaround appears as an entrypoint script, Helm template, init container, or CI interpolation step on other platforms. As a side note, 42 modules can inline secrets into the world-readable /nix/store. That direct security problem is tracked in nixpkgs issue #24288. The 110 runtime mergers make the broader point: even when deployment authors avoid the leak, the missing separation still creates work. Give secrets their own interfaceSection titled “Give secrets their own interface” Applications should accept secret values through a dedicated runtime channel, such as: a password_file or token_file setting; a systemd credential; a narrowly scoped environment variable; or an external secret provider. These mechanisms are not equally safe: environment variables can be inherited, arguments can appear in process listings, and files still need correct permissions. What separation does guarantee is that the deployer no longer has to manufacture a second, secret-bearing version of the configuration. The principle is simple; implementing it across environments is not. Local development might use a system keyring, CI environment variables, and production 1Password or Vault. Without a shared abstraction, each environment needs its own naming, lookup, validation, and injection glue. How I got it wrong in CachixSection titled “How I got it wrong in Cachix” Cachix historically stored its auth token and per-cache signing keys in ~/.config/cachix/cachix.dhall, alongside cache names and other configuration. It was convenient, but the file had to be treated as a secret even though much of it was ordinary configuration. A typical file mixed them directly: { authToken = "XXX-AUTH-TOKEN", binaryCaches = [ { name = "mycache" , secretKey = "XXX-SIGNING-KEY" } ]} The cache name is configuration; the auth token and signing key are secrets. You could not share the cache configuration without also sharing credentials. devenv 2.2 separates the token through SecretSpec. The project declares CACHIX_AUTH_TOKEN, devenv resolves it from the configured provider, and the value is passed to Cachix without being added to devenv’s configuration. Cachix PR #737 brings the same boundary into the client through the SecretSpec Haskell SDK. It resolves CACHIX_AUTH_TOKEN and CACHIX_SIGNING_KEY from SecretSpec and can store them in the user’s chosen provider instead of cachix.dhall. Existing environment variables and config files remain higher-priority fallbacks for compatibility. The PR is still open and is not available in a released Cachix version yet. That is the problem SecretSpec is designed to solve: configuration declares the requirement, while each environment chooses where the value lives. Declare once, resolve anywhereSection titled “Declare once, resolve anywhere” SecretSpec applies that separation by making secretspec.toml a declaration of what an application needs, without storing the values: [project]name = "myapp"[profiles.production]DATABASE_URL = { description = "Postgres connection string" }STRIPE_API_KEY = { description = "Stripe secret key" } Providers decide where the values live. A developer can use the system keyring, CI can use environment variables, and production can use 1Password, Vault/OpenBao, or a cloud secret manager without changing the declaration. An existing application can receive the resolved values at startup: secretspec run -- ./myapp Applications can also resolve them directly through the SecretSpec SDKs for Rust, Python, Go, Ruby, Node.js/TypeScript, Haskell, PHP, and C#, all sharing the same resolver so behavior stays consistent across languages. Providers own where secret values come from. SDKs give applications an idiomatic way to consume them. Configuration remains a shareable declaration of what is required. Making the boundary practicalSection titled “Making the boundary practical” If you maintain an application, stop adding passwords and tokens to ordinary configuration schemas. Accept a file reference, credential, environment variable, or provider instead. For NixOS, SecretSpec issue #65 tracks how an official integration could declare and resolve secrets without per-module substitution glue. Consistent secret handling across developer machines, CI, and production used to require infrastructure that only dedicated platform teams could build. A project of any size should be able to separate secrets from configuration without building its own secrets platform first.

blocks.html_en: Consistent secret handling across developer machines, CI, and production used to require infrastructure that only dedicated platform teams could build. A project of any size should be able to separate secrets from configuration without building its own secrets platform first.

content_html_zh:
Jul 20, 2026

应用程序不应在其配置文件中要求密码、API 密钥或令牌。

配置描述了行为。 它属于 git、代码审查、错误报告和开发机器。

秘密赋予权力。 它需要限制访问和独立轮换。

将两者放在一个文件中可以耦合不同的生命周期和受众。 如果轮换密码需要重新生成应用程序配置，则说明接口将它们耦合得太紧密。

NixOS 包含 110 个解决方法

标题为“NixOS 包含 110 个解决方法”的部分

我们审核了提交 141f212 处 nixpkgs 中处理真实秘密的所有 445 个 NixOS 模块，并根据其秘密值的最终位置对每个模块进行分类。

秘密值最终在哪里模块Share
在运行时合并到配置文件中11025%
内联到 /nix/store 中的配置中429%
作为环境变量提供16136%
留在应用程序打开的专用文件中5813%
通过 systemd 凭据加载5312%
作为命令行参数传递194%
分类不确定2—

有趣的数字是 110。 四分之一的模块安全地检索秘密，然后将其复制到配置中，因为这是应用程序接受的唯一接口。

这些模块使用 envsubst、replace-secret、jq、yq、sed 或自定义代码在启动时组装受限文件。 结果可能是安全的，但每个模块现在都拥有特定于应用程序的、安全敏感的粘合剂，只是为了组合两个本应保持独立的输入。

这并不是 NixOS 独有的。 相同的解决方法在其他平台上显示为入口点脚本、Helm 模板、init 容器或 CI 插值步骤。

顺便说一句，42 个模块可以将秘密内联到世界可读的 /nix/store 中。 nixpkgs 问题 #24288 中跟踪了该直接安全问题。 110 次运行时合并提出了更广泛的观点：即使部署作者避免了泄漏，缺失的分离仍然会创造工作。

为秘密提供自己的界面

标题为“为秘密提供自己的界面”的部分

应用程序应通过专用的运行时通道接受秘密值，例如：

  • 密码文件或令牌文件设置；
  • 系统凭证；
  • 范围狭窄的环境变量；
  • 或外部秘密提供者。

这些机制并不同样安全：环境变量可以继承，参数可以出现在进程列表中，文件仍然需要正确的权限。 分离确实保证了部署者不再需要制造第二个包含秘密的配置版本。

原理很简单；跨环境实施则不然。 本地开发可能会使用系统密钥环、CI 环境变量和生产 1Password 或 Vault。 如果没有共享抽象，每个环境都需要自己的命名、查找、验证和注入粘合。

我是如何在 Cachix 中出错的

标题为“我如何在 Cachix 中出错”的部分

Cachix 历史上将其身份验证令牌和每个缓存签名密钥与缓存名称和其他配置一起存储在 ~/.config/cachix/cachix.dhall 中。 这很方便，但即使其中大部分是普通配置，该文件也必须被视为秘密。

一个典型的文件直接混合它们：

缓存名称为configuration；身份验证令牌和签名密钥是秘密。 如果不共享凭据，则无法共享缓存配置。

devenv 2.2通过SecretSpec分离token。 该项目声明了 CACHIX_AUTH_TOKEN，devenv 从配置的提供程序中解析它，并将该值传递给 Cachix，而不添加到 devenv 的配置中。

Cachix PR #737 通过 SecretSpec Haskell SDK 将相同的边界引入客户端。 它从 SecretSpec 解析 CACHIX_AUTH_TOKEN 和 CACHIX_SIGNING_KEY ，并可以将它们存储在用户选择的提供程序中，而不是 cachix.dhall 中。 现有的环境变量和配置文件仍然是兼容性的更高优先级后备。 该 PR 仍处于开放状态，并且在已发布的 Cachix 版本中尚未提供。

这就是 SecretSpec 旨在解决的问题：配置声明需求，而每个环境选择值所在的位置。

声明一次，随处解决

标题为“一次声明，随处解决”的部分

SecretSpec 通过使 Secretspec.toml 声明应用程序所需内容来应用这种分离，而不存储值：

提供者决定价值所在。 开发人员可以使用系统密钥环，CI 可以使用环境变量，生产可以使用 1Password、Vault/OpenBao 或云秘密管理器，而无需更改声明。

现有应用程序可以在启动时接收解析值：

应用程序还可以通过适用于 Rust、Python、Go、Ruby、Node.js/TypeScript、Haskell、PHP 和 C# 的 SecretSpec SDK 直接解析它们，所有这些都共享相同的解析器，因此跨语言的行为保持一致。

提供商拥有秘密价值的来源。 SDK 为应用程序提供了一种惯用的使用方式。 配置仍然是所需内容的可共享声明。

使边界切实可行

标题为“使边界切实可行”的部分

如果您维护应用程序，请停止向普通配置模式添加密码和令牌。 接受文件引用、凭据、环境变量或提供程序。

对于 NixOS，SecretSpec 问题 #65 跟踪官方集成如何在没有每个模块替换胶水的情况下声明和解析机密。

跨开发人员机器、CI 和生产的一致秘密处理过去需要只有专用平台团队才能构建的基础设施。 任何规模的项目都应该能够将秘密与配置分开，而无需首先构建自己的秘密平台。
